title: Accounting: The 3 Statements
description: Learn what the income statement, balance sheet, and cash flow statement actually are — in plain English, with real-world examples. No prior accounting knowledge needed.

sections.title: Why do three statements even exist?
sections.body:
Imagine you started a small coffee cart. After a few months, a family member asks: 'How's the business going?'

You could answer in three different ways:
• 'I'm making money — my sales are way higher than my costs.'
• 'I own two carts and an espresso machine, and I owe $2,000 on the business credit card.'
• 'My bank account has grown $800 this month.'

Each answer is measuring something different. The first is about profit. The second is about what you own and owe. The third is about actual cash.

That's exactly why there are three financial statements — because one number doesn't tell the full story of a business. Each statement answers a different question:
• Income statement: 'Did we make money this period?'
• Balance sheet: 'What do we own and owe right now?'
• Cash flow statement: 'How did real cash move?'

In investment banking, you'll use all three constantly. Understanding them deeply — not just memorizing them — is what separates good analysts from great ones.
keyPoints: Three statements exist because no single number tells the full story
Income statement = profitability over a period
Balance sheet = snapshot of what you own and owe at one moment in time
Cash flow statement = how actual cash moved in and out

sections.title: The Income Statement — 'Did we make money?'
sections.body:
The income statement is the simplest to understand intuitively. It starts at the top with money coming in, and subtracts costs until you get to what's left.

Let's use a coffee cart example:

Revenue: $10,000 (you sold $10,000 worth of coffee this month)
COGS (Cost of Goods Sold): −$3,000 (beans, cups, milk)
Gross Profit: $7,000

Operating Expenses: −$4,000 (rent for your spot, employee wages, your own phone)
EBITDA: $3,000

D&A (Depreciation): −$200 (your espresso machine slowly loses value over time)
EBIT (Operating Income): $2,800

Interest Expense: −$100 (interest on the $2,000 loan)
EBT (Earnings Before Tax): $2,700

Tax: −$540 (20% tax rate)
Net Income: $2,160

Net income — the 'bottom line' — is what the company earned after everything. It's the most-watched number in earnings season.

One key thing: the income statement covers a period of time — a month, quarter, or year. It's not a snapshot; it's more like a report card for that stretch of time.
keyPoints: Revenue − COGS = Gross Profit (profit before overhead)
Gross Profit − Operating Expenses = EBITDA
EBITDA − D&A = EBIT (Operating Income)
EBIT − Interest − Tax = Net Income (the 'bottom line')
The income statement covers a time period, not a single moment

sections.title: The Balance Sheet — 'What do we own and owe?'
sections.body:
The balance sheet is a photo of the business at a single point in time. It shows everything the company owns (assets), everything it owes (liabilities), and what's left over for the owners (equity).

The golden rule: Assets = Liabilities + Equity. This always holds — it's like saying 'everything you own was paid for either by borrowing or by your own money.'

For the coffee cart:

Assets (what we own):
• Cash: $3,500
• Inventory (beans, cups): $400
• Equipment (espresso machine): $4,000
Total Assets: $7,900

Liabilities (what we owe):
• Business credit card balance: $2,000
Total Liabilities: $2,000

Equity (owner's stake):
• Total Assets − Total Liabilities = $7,900 − $2,000 = $5,900

Equity is what the business is 'worth' to the owner in an accounting sense. It grows when the business makes profits (because net income flows into retained earnings, which is part of equity).

In real companies, assets are split into current (cash within a year) and non-current (long-term like equipment). Same for liabilities: current means due within a year (like next month's rent), non-current means long-term debt.
keyPoints: Assets = Liabilities + Equity — this always balances, on every transaction
Assets: everything the company owns (cash, equipment, inventory)
Liabilities: everything it owes (loans, unpaid bills, credit lines)
Equity: the owner's residual stake — what's left after paying all obligations
The balance sheet is a snapshot at one moment in time, not over a period

sections.title: The Cash Flow Statement — 'Do we have real cash?'
sections.body:
Here's the twist: profit doesn't equal cash. A business can show a profit on paper while having very little cash in the bank — or vice versa.

Why? A few reasons:
• You sold $1,000 of catering services but won't collect payment for 30 days → profit recorded, cash not yet received
• You bought a $4,000 espresso machine → cash went out, but it's not an 'expense' immediately (it's an asset that depreciates slowly)
• You depreciate the machine $200/month → income goes down, but no cash leaves

The cash flow statement untangles this. It reconciles your profit back to actual cash. It has three sections:

1. Cash from Operations (CFO): Starts with net income, then adds back non-cash charges (like depreciation) and adjusts for timing differences (like waiting to collect from customers). This is the core health metric of the business.

2. Cash from Investing (CFI): Cash spent on buying equipment, buildings, or other long-term assets. Buying the espresso machine is here.

3. Cash from Financing (CFF): Cash from borrowing money, paying it back, or exchanging with investors. Taking out the $2,000 loan goes here.

Ending Cash = Beginning Cash + CFO + CFI + CFF. And that ending cash matches the cash line on the balance sheet — that's one of the key connections between the statements.
keyPoints: Profit ≠ cash — timing and non-cash items cause the difference
CFO: cash from the core business (net income adjusted for non-cash and timing)
CFI: cash from buying or selling long-term assets (like equipment)
CFF: cash from borrowing, repaying debt, or raising equity
Ending cash on the CFS = cash on the balance sheet (key linkage)

sections.title: How the three statements connect
sections.body:
This is the most important thing to understand — and the most common interview question. The three statements aren't separate; they're wired together.

Here's the flow:

1. Net income from the Income Statement flows to two places:
   → Cash Flow Statement: it's the starting point for CFO
   → Balance Sheet: it increases Retained Earnings (part of equity)

2. Depreciation from the Income Statement:
   → Gets added back in CFO (it's non-cash)
   → Reduces PP&E on the Balance Sheet over time

3. CapEx (equipment purchases) from the Cash Flow Statement:
   → Shows as a cash outflow in CFI
   → Increases PP&E on the Balance Sheet

4. Ending cash from the Cash Flow Statement:
   → Must equal the Cash line on the Balance Sheet

Let's test it with a transaction: 'Depreciation goes up by $10.'
• Income statement: EBIT and net income both fall by $10. After 40% tax, net income falls $6.
• Cash flow: starts $6 lower (net income), but add back $10 D&A (non-cash). CFO is up $4. Cash goes up $4.
• Balance sheet: PP&E falls $10 (more depreciation). Cash rises $4. Retained earnings falls $6. Check: Assets change = −$10 + $4 = −$6. Equity change = −$6. Balanced.

This type of walk-through is an extremely common interview question. Practice it until it's automatic.
keyPoints: Net income → retained earnings (BS) and starting point of CFS
D&A: non-cash expense on IS, added back in CFO, reduces PP&E on BS
CapEx: cash outflow in CFI, increases PP&E on BS
Ending cash on CFS = cash on BS — the key tie-out
Any IS change flows through to equity via retained earnings

sections.title: Terms you'll hear constantly — plain English
sections.body:
Here's a glossary of the terms that come up most. These aren't definitions to memorize — they're concepts to internalize.

COGS (Cost of Goods Sold): The direct cost to make or deliver what you sold. For a coffee cart, it's beans and cups. For a software company, it might be server costs and customer support.

Gross Margin: Gross Profit divided by Revenue. Shows how efficiently you make your product. A 70% gross margin means you keep $0.70 of every $1 in sales after direct costs.

EBITDA: Earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization. Used to compare companies across different debt structures or tax situations. Think of it as 'operating cash flow before the fine print.'

Working Capital: Current assets minus current liabilities (usually excluding cash and debt). Represents money tied up in day-to-day operations. High working capital means more cash locked up in inventory or receivables.

Free Cash Flow (FCF): Cash from operations minus CapEx. The purest measure of cash the business actually generates — what's left for investors after running the business and maintaining assets.

Retained Earnings: The accumulation of all net income the company has earned, minus all dividends paid to shareholders. It's how the income statement history lives on the balance sheet.
keyPoints: COGS: direct cost of making the product/service
EBITDA: operating profit before interest, taxes, D&A — used for company comparisons
Working capital: money tied up in operations (current assets minus current liabilities)
FCF = CFO − CapEx: the truest measure of cash generation
Retained earnings: accumulated net income on the balance sheet
